## [00:00 - 03:01]

Let's talk about offline conversions. First of all, what are they offline conversions are your your ability to send more data to Facebook in terms of your sales or any events but for us for e-commerce its purchases that Facebook may not have captured. Now originally this was designed for brick and mortar stores that would also run online advertising for them to track sales that were generated through Facebook ads but ultimately converted in store. How does that help us in e-commerce? Well with the lack of tracking we can fill in some of the gaps and I'll show you in a separate video how you can actually analyze that and the power of doing this but for now I want to show you a few different ways to get this set up. So first of all you can do it manually and manually allows you to download data from your sales database. Could be Shopify, could be any other platform and upload it on a daily or weekly or a monthly basis but also there's a way to automate that as well and I'd really recommend you do so. Now this link I'll share with this below the training so that you have the link. I'm not going to break this down and kind of show you the exact because Facebook changes from time to time. It's better that you come to this link and find out how to actually create your offline event set. So in short go to events manager, select connect data sources, select offline and then get started. You need to give your offline event set a name. This is what will appear when you set up when you're looking at your pixel also when you're looking inside your business manager and also at the ad level to show what offline event set has been set up. Click create, attach it to the ad accounts and you can change this later as well. Turn on auto tracking so that offline event data is set up automatically for matching and tracking and then you've got the ability to upload that data. Now the event data that can be uploaded is also linked on this page. What's interesting and by the way this can change from time to time you have 90 days from the ad impression to upload that data. Now what that means is if data had come in beyond that 90 days it won't be tracked or included. If it's within 90 days from the ad impression then Facebook will include it in its analysis and we would recommend that you do it for at least the last 30 days and do it on a rolling basis. You can upload historical data up to 90 days for analysis but beyond that you can't see any past data as well. So make sure you go to the maximum of 30 when you first do it and then make sure as Facebook recommend that you do it daily. This is a recommendation so that the data is fresh. Now when that data is sent from your sales database you want to make sure that you're setting certain data in your uploads such as email address, date and time of purchase and giving as much data to Facebook as possible. So you'll

## [03:01 - 06:04]

get a view similar to this and it will say you know select your CSV. You want to send that data through that's going to give Facebook the maximum chance of matching up a user. So email and phone are two of the most basic ones. Address, postcode can help, date of birth, gender and anything else which might help identify that user. Essentially what you're doing is if this person is purchased in the Facebook pixel or the server API, the conversion API has not picked up this user as purchasing from your ad, you want Facebook to try and match it another way. This typically can happen if let's say you know the user is opted out with trackings, there's no pixels, they've got no cookie installed and for some reason the conversion API has also not picked up the data which does happen. Then any data that's been put into our database we want to see if that traffic came through Facebook. So the more data that you send to Facebook, the more chance of being able to match up that user in its own data. What then you can do and I'll show in a separate video is to analyze cells that have come through your pixel versus cells that have come through outside of the pixel and you can look at the difference not just at the overall level but at the campaign ad set and ad level as well to see if for example you're making the right decision on the data because if you just run the pixel and you're not getting all the data through you might not be making the right decision. So the first thing is to make sure you have an offline event set created, upload your first 90 days worth of data manually if you don't have it already but also bear in mind there are different methods of getting that data into Facebook so you can automate it. So in this particular account we have it automated via a third party so all of the data that the third party sees it will fire it back in. This particular third party will look at cells that are coming through PayPal, Stripe, I think it falls through Shopify and any other sources that it can so it's just trying to collect any data that it can to see if anything matched up with Facebook. The other option is that you you can code it directly against the API yourself. So in the Facebook for developers website and this link will be shared below as well you can code it yourself and the full instructions are here. This is suitable if you want to go super advanced and you've got someone technical to do this for you because you can highly customize it and tailor this for yourself. Also just as an FYI this is a great place to understand some of the Facebook products because the technical side often opens up some insights which are not available from the kind of more business focused side of the different tools that Facebook offer. I often like to come in here even though I might not understand half of it, half of it I do understand it gives me other ideas on how

## [06:04 - 07:19]

to use these tools as well so don't shy away from the fact that it is technical if you're not technical if you are great but just at least try and understand the concepts behind what's happening. That said there are also other ways as well so for example Zapier and there's Integromat and other connectors as well allow you to do that automatically so how does it connect when this happens so new paid order occurs send an offline event. What this does in particular and why I like this is it connects directly to Shopify and if a transaction happens on Shopify because that is your source of truth then it pushes it to Facebook. Guess what doesn't have to be Shopify it could be click funnels it could be WooCommerce it could be Magento it could be whatever your back end tool is where transactions are happening and it doesn't even have to be purchases it could be leads or whatever it can go into your offline conversion set so that you can see if that data is matching up so whilst I showed you you can do it manually and if it's the first time do it manually get 90 days of data in but then automate it there's no point doing it manually yourself every single day let the tech take over set and forget it and then you can focus on analysis and decision making and making more money from Facebook
